title: Data Manipulation Language (DML)
summary: Manipulate data with INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE in MySQL. Includes full syntax, practical examples with before/after tables, and the importance of COMMIT and WHERE clauses.
content_html:
Data Manipulation Language (DML)

DML is a SQL script used to change data in existing tables. The three core commands are INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE.

1. INSERT — Add a new line

Full syntax

INSERT INTO table_name (col1, col2, col3, ...)
VALUES (val1, val2, val3, ...);

Example from case study — Staff table

Before INSERT — the Staff table is empty:

StaffIDStaffNameGenderBranchID
(no rows)
 
INSERT INTO Staff (StaffID, StaffName, Gender, BranchID)
VALUES (1, 'Alice Brown', 'F', 101);

INSERT INTO Staff (StaffID, StaffName, Gender, BranchID)
VALUES (2, 'Bob Smith', 'M', 102);

INSERT INTO Staff (StaffID, StaffName, BranchID)
VALUES (3, 'Kim Lee', 103);

Sau khi INSERT:

StaffIDStaffNameGenderBranchID
1Alice BrownF101
2Bob SmithM102
3Kim LeeOther (DEFAULT)103

Line 3 does not have Gender → use the DEFAULT value 'Other'.

INSERT multiple lines at the same time

INSERT INTO Staff (StaffID, StaffName, Gender, BranchID)
VALUES (4, 'David Tan', 'M', 101),
       (5, 'Emma Wilson', 'F', 102),
       (6, 'Frank Chen', 'M', 103);

2. UPDATE — Edit existing data

Syntax

UPDATE table_name
SET col1 = val1, col2 = val2, ...
WHERE condition;

Example: Update a column

Before UPDATE — Alice Brown at BranchID 101:

UPDATE Staff
SET BranchID = 104
WHERE StaffID = 1;

Sau UPDATE — Alice Brown moves to BranchID 104.

For example: Update multiple columns at the same time

UPDATE Staff
SET StaffName = 'Alice Johnson',
    Gender = 'F',
    BranchID = 105
WHERE StaffID = 1;
⚠️ DANGER: Forgetting WHERE in UPDATE!
UPDATE Staff SET BranchID = 104;
→ This command updates ALL row in the Staff table! There is no way to undo without using a transaction.

3. DELETE — Delete data

Syntax

DELETE FROM table_name
WHERE condition;
COMMIT;

Example: Delete an employee

Before DELETE — 3 employees on the board.

DELETE FROM Staff
WHERE StaffID = 3;
COMMIT;

Sau DELETE — Kim Lee (StaffID=3) has been deleted, leaving only Alice and Bob.

⚠️ DANGER: Forget WHERE in DELETE!
DELETE FROM Staff;
→ Delete WHOLE data in the Staff table! COMMIT will make this impossible to undo.

4. Bind Foreign Key when DELETE

If the table has a Foreign Key reference, you must delete the data in the correct order (child first, parent later):

DELETE FROM PropertyInspection
WHERE StaffID = 3;

DELETE FROM Staff
WHERE StaffID = 3;

COMMIT;

If you delete Staff before deleting PropertyInspection, MySQL will report an error of violating the FK constraint.

5. COMMIT — Confirm changes

Command COMMIT Permanently commits DML changes to the database. You should:

  • Check the results before COMMIT
  • Use ROLLBACK if an error is detected (before COMMIT)
  • Only COMMIT when sure the data is correct
INSERT INTO Staff (StaffID, StaffName, Gender, BranchID)
VALUES (7, 'Grace Park', 'F', 101);

SELECT * FROM Staff WHERE StaffID = 7;

COMMIT;

6. AUTO_INCREMENT

Instead of managing IDs yourself, use AUTO_INCREMENT:

CREATE TABLE PropertyInspection (
    InspectionID INT NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT,
    PropertyID   INT NOT NULL,
    InspDate     DATE NOT NULL,
    PRIMARY KEY (InspectionID)
);

INSERT INTO PropertyInspection (PropertyID, InspDate)
VALUES (201, '2025-03-15');

InspectionID will automatically be 1, 2, 3... without needing to be provided.
